contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Threshold objectives can support a monotonic moving boundary under suitable value constraints, while exact-sum objectives often require matching cumulative-state differences; equality wording alone never proves a sliding window.

items.prompt: All values are positive, and the task asks for the minimum length with sum at least k. Which objective signal favors a variable window?

options.text: The threshold becomes true monotonically as right expands, and shrinking searches shorter valid intervals.
The answer requires an earlier prefix equal to currentPrefix - k.
The values should be sorted before choosing a range.
Every problem containing a sum uses the same method.

feedback.reason: The inequality and positive values create a validity region that can be repaired and minimized by moving boundaries.
feedback.details: This is different from matching one exact cumulative difference.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_prefix: That relation is central to exact equality, not necessary for this positive threshold objective.

items.prompt: Values may be negative, and the task asks whether any subarray sums exactly to k. Which signal favors prefix-hash state?

options.text: Exact equality is expressed as currentPrefix - earlierPrefix = k without relying on monotone boundary moves.
Shrink when sum is above k and expand when below k.
Treat all negative values as zero.
Sort values and search adjacent blocks.

feedback.reason: Exact sum is a relation between two cumulative boundaries.
feedback.details: Hashing earlier prefixes supports the relation for arbitrary signs.

items.prompt: All values are nonnegative, and the task asks for existence of an exact sum k. Which conclusion is correct?

options.text: A carefully defined window can work, while prefix methods also remain correct; workload and edge cases decide.
Exact equality always excludes sliding window.
The same window is correct for arbitrary signed values.
Exact equality cannot be solved on contiguous ranges.

feedback.reason: The words exact sum do not uniquely determine a strategy.
feedback.details: The value domain and required reuse distinguish a nonnegative window from a more general prefix representation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_only: Nonnegative sum movement can support equality search.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_method: Both mechanisms can solve it under suitable contracts.

items.prompt: A nonnegative window has just been shrunk until its sum is at most the budget. Why should its current length be evaluated before right advances?

options.text: After shrinking any over-budget interval, record the current valid length.
Record only when sum equals budget.
Return the first valid interval.
Look only for prefix difference exactly equal to budget.

feedback.reason: The objective accepts every sum below or equal to the budget.
feedback.details: After monotone repair, the current interval is the longest valid one ending at right.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.record_only_equal: A longest at-most answer may have a smaller sum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_match: The inequality allows many valid sums.

items.prompt: A static array receives many queries for the exact sum of a specified interval [l, r]. Which method is natural?

options.text: Use a prefix difference for the already specified boundaries.
Move boundaries until the sum happens to equal the query answer.
Use a frequency map to discover an unknown start.
Sort each interval before summing.

feedback.reason: Exact can describe either a target value or the arithmetic value of a known interval.
feedback.details: Here prefix subtraction answers the requested fixed boundaries directly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.search_window: The query already supplies the boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort: Order is irrelevant to sum but sorting changes the interval representation.

items.prompt: Values may be negative, and the task asks for the shortest interval with sum at least k. What is the safe conclusion?

options.text: The simple threshold window is invalid; prefix sums with an order-aware structure may be required.
A frequency map for prefix - k always gives the shortest at-least interval.
Use the positive-value shrink rule unchanged.
Sort and use two pointers.

feedback.reason: Threshold versus exact is only one axis; sign constraints are another.
feedback.details: For this mixed-sign inequality, prefix sums combined with a monotonic deque are a known appropriate mechanism.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_hash_frequency: The objective is inequality and shortest length, not exact equality count.

items.prompt: Which phrase most directly changes an exact-sum problem into a threshold problem?

options.text: "sum at least k" instead of "sum exactly k."
Adding the word contiguous.
Calling the sequence an array.
Using the word target.

feedback.reason: Equality selects one aggregate value; a threshold selects a range of valid values.
feedback.details: That changes answer timing and the boundary rule when monotonicity exists.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.contiguous: Both problem types already concern contiguous intervals.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array: Data-container vocabulary does not change objective semantics.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target: Both exact and threshold objectives can have targets.

items.prompt: The result asks for how many subarrays sum exactly to k. Why is a minimum-length threshold window the wrong abstraction?

options.text: Counting every exact match requires preserving all matching start boundaries, not only one current valid window.
Counts cannot refer to contiguous intervals.
A window cannot maintain a sum.
Prefix values must be sorted first.

feedback.reason: The result contract requires multiplicity of exact cumulative differences.
feedback.details: A single moving left boundary discards earlier starts that may each form distinct matches.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_no_sum: It can, but its state does not retain all exact starts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_sorted: Hash frequencies can count in scan order.

items.prompt: All values are positive. The task asks for the maximum length with sum exactly k. Which statement is most accurate?

options.text: A monotone window can search equality, but zeros are absent and answer timing must retain the longest matching state.
Exact equality is identical to sum at most k.
Prefix differences cannot represent exact sums.
An exact-sum window requires negative values.

feedback.reason: Strict positivity creates monotone sum movement even for an equality objective.
feedback.details: The algorithm must distinguish valid equality from merely below or above the target.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.threshold_same: At-most intervals with smaller sums are not answers.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negative_required: Negative values weaken the simple window.

options.text: Choose sliding window whenever the prompt mentions a contiguous sum.
For a threshold, check whether value signs create monotone boundary effects.
For exact sums with arbitrary signs, consider historical prefix lookup.
For many fixed-boundary queries, consider precomputed prefix differences.

feedback.reason: The same surface words cover several different search and query contracts.
feedback.details: Strategy follows objective, signs, boundary ownership, and reuse.

items.prompt: Which facts support a threshold sliding window? Select all that apply.

options.text: The answer uses at-least or at-most validity rather than one exact value.
Value constraints make expansion and shrinking directional.
The scan can maintain one current candidate interval.
Values are arbitrary signed with no stronger invariant.

feedback.reason: Threshold wording is useful only together with a monotone value domain and matching boundary search.
feedback.details: Arbitrary signs break the simple form.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_negatives: The simple sum window loses its discard rule.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.inequality: A validity region can be maintained.

items.prompt: Which facts support exact-sum prefix state? Select all that apply.

options.text: A valid interval satisfies currentPrefix - earlierPrefix = k.
Values may be positive, zero, or negative.
Counting or optimizing may require information about several earlier boundaries.
Only the first threshold-valid interval matters.

feedback.reason: Prefix state retains historical boundary information for exact equality.
feedback.details: A first threshold-valid interval does not describe that requirement.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_valid_only: That is a different result contract.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_signs: The identity does not need monotonicity.

items.prompt: Which tasks can admit either a window or prefix method under nonnegative values? Select all that apply.

options.text: Existence of an exact-sum interval.
All length-k interval sums.
Maximum length-k sum.
Shortest sum-at-least interval using only the ordinary shrink rule with arbitrary negatives.

feedback.reason: Some contracts leave both strategies legal.
feedback.details: Space, reuse, and generality then decide; mixed-sign threshold correctness does not.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_exists: A window can exploit monotonicity; prefix lookup is also correct.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_fixed_windows: A rolling sum or prefix differences both work.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.max_fixed_window: Both can enumerate the same candidates.

items.prompt: Which edge cases clarify the threshold-versus-exact boundary? Select all that apply.

options.text: Zeros that create several windows with the same sum.
Intervals exceed k but none equal k.
Several exact matches with different lengths.
Only one case where exact and threshold happen to return the same interval.

feedback.reason: Good tests separate the accepted aggregate set and the output objective.
feedback.details: A coincidental shared answer does not prove the methods are interchangeable.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.zeros: They affect equality and minimization timing.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_but_not_min: The result may need count, shortest, or longest metadata.

items.prompt: Which mistaken claims collapse threshold and exact-sum objectives into one rule? Select all that apply.

options.text: Exact sum can never use a sliding window.
Every threshold objective supports a simple window.
Contiguity alone decides between the methods.
Exact subarray sum is a prefix difference.

feedback.reason: Objective labels must be combined with value and workload constraints.
feedback.details: The prefix identity remains universally valid for sums.

items.prompt: Arrange the strategy decision for a contiguous-sum objective.

elements.text: State exact, at-least, at-most, count, shortest, longest, or fixed-boundary semantics.
State whether values are positive, nonnegative, or arbitrary signed.
State one sequential scan versus repeated interval queries.
Choose moving, prefix-array, prefix-hash, or stronger prefix state from those constraints.

feedback.reason: The method follows three independent dimensions: result, value domain, and access pattern.
feedback.details: The word sum does not collapse them into one template.

elements.text: Represent every subarray by two prefix boundaries.
Rewrite exact sum k as earlierPrefix = currentPrefix - k.
Choose existence, frequency, earliest, or latest metadata from the output contract.
Accumulate prefixes and query prior state before storing the current boundary.

feedback.reason: Exact equality becomes a historical boundary lookup.
feedback.details: The payload is determined by whether the result is existence, count, shortest, or longest.

items.prompt: Compare two legal O(n) strategies: a nonnegative threshold window storing only sum and boundaries, and an arbitrary-signed exact-sum prefix-hash scan.
constraints: The prefix hash may retain n distinct prefix values.

feedback.reason: Both can be linear under their respective contracts, but they solve different objective and value-domain problems.
feedback.details: The prefix-hash path spends memory to avoid relying on monotone boundary movement.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Threshold objectives can support a monotonic moving boundary under suitable value constraints, while exact-sum objectives often require matching cumulative-state differences; equality wording alone never proves a sliding window.
